label: Uruk (Warka)
sub: World's first city (c. 3500 BCE); birthplace of cuneiform writing and urban civilization. Population reached 50,000 at peak.

label: Ur (Tell el-Mukayyar)
sub: Sumerian city-state; Third Dynasty of Ur (2112–2004 BCE) created the world's first bureaucratic state. Woolley excavations 1922–1934.

label: Nippur
sub: Sacred city of Enlil, supreme god of the Sumerian pantheon. Religious center for millennia; yielded over 30,000 cuneiform tablets in US excavations (1889–1900).

label: Babylon
sub: Capital of Hammurabi's empire (1792–1750 BCE) and Nebuchadnezzar II's Neo-Babylonian empire (605–562 BCE). Site of the legendary Hanging Gardens. Koldewey excavation 1899.

label: Nineveh
sub: Capital of the Neo-Assyrian Empire under Ashurbanipal (668–627 BCE). Site of the Library of Ashurbanipal with 30,000+ cuneiform tablets. Layard excavation 1847.

label: Ashur (Qal'at Sherqat)
sub: Original capital and religious center of the Assyrian Empire. UNESCO World Heritage Site. German excavation 1903–1914; threatened by ISIS demolitions 2015.

label: Ctesiphon (Taq Kasra)
sub: Sasanian imperial capital (224–637 CE); largest pre-industrial city in the world at its peak (~500,000). The Taq Kasra arch remains the world's largest single-span brick vault.

label: Baghdad
sub: Capital of Iraq since 762 CE. Founded by Abbasid Caliph al-Mansur as the 'Round City.' Current seat of the federal government under PM al-Sudani.

label: Erbil (Hawler)
sub: Capital of Kurdistan Region of Iraq (KRI). Continuously inhabited for 6,000+ years — one of the world's oldest cities. Seat of KRG under President Nechirvan Barzani.

label: Mosul (Nineveh Province)
sub: Iraq's second-largest city; capital of Nineveh Province. Captured by ISIS June 2014; liberated July 2017 in the Battle of Mosul. Ongoing reconstruction.

label: Basra
sub: Iraq's main port city and oil export hub. Founded in 638 CE. Contains the largest concentration of Iraq's oil fields; frequent protests over poor services and unemployment.

label: Sulaymaniyah
sub: Second-largest city in the Kurdistan Region; stronghold of the Patriotic Union of Kurdistan (PUK). Known for relative press freedom and cultural openness.

label: Kirkuk
sub: Disputed city claimed by both federal Iraq and the Kurdistan Region. Sits atop major oil fields. Under KRG control 2014–2017; retaken by federal forces October 2017.

label: Najaf
sub: Shia Islam's holiest city; contains the Imam Ali Mosque and the world's largest cemetery (Wadi al-Salam). Seat of Grand Ayatollah Ali al-Sistani.

label: Karbala
sub: Site of the Battle of Karbala (680 CE) and martyrdom of Imam Hussein. One of Shia Islam's holiest cities; draws 20+ million pilgrims annually for Arba'een.

label: Battle of al-Qadisiyya (636 CE)
sub: Arab Muslim forces defeated the Sasanian Persian army here in 636 CE, ending Persian control of Mesopotamia and opening Iraq to Islamic conquest.

label: Battle of Karbala (680 CE)
sub: Imam Hussein ibn Ali (grandson of Prophet Muhammad) was killed here on October 10, 680 CE (Ashura) by Umayyad forces. The foundational event of the Shia-Sunni split.

label: Mongol Sack of Baghdad (1258)
sub: Hulagu Khan's Mongol forces sacked Baghdad February 10–13, 1258, killing the Abbasid Caliph and massacring the population. Ended the Islamic Golden Age.

label: Battle of Fallujah (Nov 2004)
sub: Operation Phantom Fury (November–December 2004): US and Iraqi forces retook Fallujah from Sunni insurgents in the bloodiest urban battle of the Iraq War. ~95 US Marines killed; 800+ insurgents; thousands of civilians displaced.

label: Battle of Tikrit (2015)
sub: Iraqi Security Forces and PMF retook Saddam Hussein's hometown of Tikrit from ISIS in March–April 2015 in the first major post-Mosul offensive. Significant PMF role amid US-Iran tensions.

label: Battle of Mosul (2016–2017)
sub: Largest urban battle of the 21st century. Over 100,000 forces vs ISIS October 2016–July 2017. ~10,000 killed; 800,000 displaced; Old City nearly destroyed. ISIS declared Caliphate from Mosul in 2014.

label: Rumaila Oil Field
sub: Iraq's largest oil field and one of the world's largest, with estimated reserves of 17 billion barrels. Operated by BP and CNPC. Produces ~1.4 million bpd. Straddles Iraq-Kuwait border (source of 1990 slant-drilling dispute).

label: West Qurna Oil Field
sub: Two major fields (West Qurna-1 operated by ExxonMobil, West Qurna-2 by Lukoil). Combined reserves of ~43 billion barrels. Produces ~800,000 bpd.

label: Majnoon Oil Field
sub: Giant oil field with estimated reserves of 38 billion barrels. Developed by Shell and Petronas. Produces ~220,000 bpd. Name means 'crazy' in Arabic — so large geologists thought the discoverer was mad.

label: Kirkuk Oil Fields
sub: Iraq's oldest major oil fields, first exploited in 1927. Estimated reserves of 9 billion barrels. Control disputed between Baghdad and Erbil; oil exports via the Iraq-Turkey Pipeline (ITP) halted since March 2023 following an arbitration ruling.

label: Basra Oil Terminal (ABOT/KAAOT)
sub: Iraq's primary crude oil export terminal in the Persian Gulf. The Al-Basrah Oil Terminal (ABOT) and Khor Al-Amaya Oil Terminal (KAAOT) handle ~90% of Iraq's oil exports. Attacked by US forces in 2003 to prevent sabotage.

label: Sinjar (Yazidi Homeland)
sub: Homeland of the Yazidi religious minority. ISIS launched a genocidal attack here August 3, 2014, killing up to 10,000 and enslaving 7,000 women and children. Recognized as genocide by the UN, US, and EU. Mass graves at 73+ locations.

label: Camp Bucca (US Detention)
sub: Largest US detention facility in Iraq (2003–2009); at peak held 26,000 detainees. Called the 'ISIS Academy' — Abu Bakr al-Baghdadi and several other ISIS founding figures were detained here. Closed 2009.

label: Mosul Dam
sub: Iraq's largest dam and a critical infrastructure asset. Built on unstable gypsum foundation requiring constant grouting. US engineers warned in 2016 that failure could send a 15-meter wave to Mosul and a 5-meter wave to Baghdad, potentially killing 500,000+.

label: Samarra — Al-Askari Mosque
sub: Site of the Al-Askari Mosque, containing the tombs of the 10th and 11th Shia Imams. Bombing of its golden dome on February 22, 2006 by AQI/ISIS triggered Iraq's sectarian civil war (2006–2008).
